coredata.dc:description: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots are gaining significant attention due to their sustainable production and multifunctionality in photocatalysis, anticounterfeiting, artificial photosynthesis, biosensing, bioimaging, and medical diagnostics. The notable health risks posed by heavy metal ions as predominant water pollutants underscore the urgent need for the advancement of highly sensitive, portable, and economically viable sensor technologies. In this study, we synthesized a dual-probe fluorescent sensor for trace metal ions detection by using multicolor carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) derived from mahogany leaves through a one-step hydrothermal process. Prepared MCQDs exhibited excellent multicolor emission, with a peak at 440 nm, and displayed dual sensitivity to Fe3+ and As3+ ions in water when excited at 340 nm. The presence of Fe3+ ions caused fluorescence quenching, enabling their detection and removal from aqueous solutions, while As3+ ions enhanced the fluorescence emission. The fluorometric sensor demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity, achieving detection limits of 8.07 nM for Fe3+ and 2.36 nM for As3+ ions in deionized water. Further, cellular studies confirm that the MCQDs effectively detected Fe3+ and As3+ ions in NIH-3T3 cell lines, exhibiting minimal cytotoxic effects. Furthermore, positive hemolysis results confirmed their biocompatibility. The live in-vivo imaging of MCQDs in mice paves the way for advancements in drug delivery systems. Thus, these findings underscore that the mahogany leaf-derived MCQDs offer a multifunctional platform as versatile dual probes for heavy metal ion detection and bioimaging applications.

head.ce:title: Biogenic synthesis of highly stable multifluorescent, multifaceted carbon quantum dots as dual probe sensor for detection of heavy toxic metal ions – Fe3 + and As3+ in water and mouse fibroblast cell line NIH-3T3, and for live in-vivo imaging in mice

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots are gaining significant attention due to their sustainable production and multifunctionality in photocatalysis, anticounterfeiting, artificial photosynthesis, biosensing, bioimaging, and medical diagnostics. The notable health risks posed by heavy metal ions as predominant water pollutants underscore the urgent need for the advancement of highly sensitive, portable, and economically viable sensor technologies. In this study, we synthesized a dual-probe fluorescent sensor for trace metal ions detection by using multicolor carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) derived from mahogany leaves through a one-step hydrothermal process. Prepared MCQDs exhibited excellent multicolor emission, with a peak at 440 nm, and displayed dual sensitivity to Fe3+ and As3+ ions in water when excited at 340 nm. The presence of Fe3+ ions caused fluorescence quenching, enabling their detection and removal from aqueous solutions, while As3+ ions enhanced the fluorescence emission. The fluorometric sensor demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity, achieving detection limits of 8.07 nM for Fe3+ and 2.36 nM for As3+ ions in deionized water. Further, cellular studies confirm that the MCQDs effectively detected Fe3+ and As3+ ions in NIH-3T3 cell lines, exhibiting minimal cytotoxic effects. Furthermore, positive hemolysis results confirmed their biocompatibility. The live in-vivo imaging of MCQDs in mice paves the way for advancements in drug delivery systems. Thus, these findings underscore that the mahogany leaf-derived MCQDs offer a multifunctional platform as versatile dual probes for heavy metal ion detection and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, as a vital resource, plays a pivotal role in sustaining life and supporting diverse ecosystems. The necessity for secure, convenient, and uncontaminated water for human consumption is paramount, yet water bodies globally face various pollutants, posing threats to both aquatic ecosystems and human health [1]. Water pollution manifests in diverse forms originating from various anthropogenic activities, including the discharge of chemical effluents, thermal and radioactive pollutants, the presence of pathogens and microbiological, inorganic, and organic pollutants, domestic wastes, and residues from agricultural practices such as insecticides [2–4]. Additionally, contaminants such as volatile organic compounds and heavy metals (Mg2+, Cr2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, As3+, As5+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, and Na+) significantly contribute to the degradation of water quality, presenting considerable environmental challenges [5,6]. Among all, heavy metals (HMs) stand out due to their persistence, toxicity, and detrimental impact on the environment. HMs are naturally found in the Earth's crust in high concentrations. They are non-biodegradable and can be toxic to humans and the environment [7]. Heavy metals can also enter water bodies through a variety of ways, including industrial waste, mining activities, and agricultural runoff. Once in the water, they can accumulate in the food chain, posing a serious threat to human health [8].

Since arsenic can exist in over 200 different chemical forms, including arsenites (AsO3 3-), arsenates (AsO4 3-), monomethyl (MMA), and dimethyl (DMA) metabolites, it poses a serious risk to human health because of its diverse oxidation states. Additionally, some arsenic-containing minerals are volatile and soluble in water, which means that they constantly contribute to the leaching of arsenic into groundwater [9,10]. Regions globally, including India, Bangladesh, China, the United States, and other countries, struggle with heightened levels of arsenic in the groundwater [11,12]. Drinking tainted water, irrigating crops with contaminated water, and preparing food with tainted water are the main ways that humans are exposed to arsenic. Beyond groundwater, fish, seafood, dairy products, pork, chicken, and other foods are also contaminated by arsenic [13]. The maximum allowable concentration of arsenic in drinking water is 10 μg/L [14]. Exceeding this limit poses significant health risks, including the development of cirrhosis, malignancies, liver fibrosis, skin lesions, parenchymal cell damage, hepatocellular carcinoma, hypertension, diabetes, lung cancer, and bladder cancer [15].

Iron, a crucial mineral in our bodies, plays a starring role in many biological processes. From carrying oxygen in red blood cells to helping enzymes work properly, it is a key player in keeping us healthy. Iron deficiency causes anemia, where our cells struggle to get enough oxygen, making us feel tired and weak. On the other hand, too much iron can build up and damage organs, increasing the risk of diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer [16,17]. The detection of iron ions in both environmental and biological systems with high sensitivity and selectivity holds considerable importance.

The detection and identification of hazardous heavy metal ions at very small concentrations in water and NIH-3T3 cell line poses a substantial challenge. While spectroscopic techniques like electrochemical methods [18], atomic absorption spectrometry, and mass spectroscopy [19] have the potential to resolve this problem, their extended analysis times, expensive nature, and bulky equipment needs render them unsuitable for on-site diagnostics. Fluorescent sensors are capable of detecting metal ions due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and spatial resolution, which allow for easy measurement and real-time monitoring [20]. Additionally, fluorescent probes enable the visualization and monitoring of metal ions in cells and organisms through imaging [21]. Commonly utilized fluorescent sensors encompass fluorescent metal nanoclusters, semiconductor quantum dots (QD), organic dyes, and fluorescent metal-organic frameworks [22]. However, there are concerns regarding their photostability, complex equipment and processing requirements, high costs, environmental impact, and potential cytotoxicity in practical applications [23]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have greater interest among researchers as bioimaging labels and sensor probes due to their exceptional attributes, including intense fluorescence, outstanding photostability [24], adjustable fluorescence emission, favorable biocompatibility [25], minimal toxicity [26], excellent water solubility [27].

Recent studies have explored the synthesis of CQDs from natural sources like orange peel [28], soya milk [29], banana Leaves [30], garlic [31], and various medicinal plants. The CQDs materials predominantly contain carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen, with various heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulfur, and many more. To enhance the properties of CQDs, their surface can be tuned by changing the various parameters like synthesis temperature, pressure, catalysts, and the incorporation of heteroatoms [32]. CQDs can be both crystalline as well as amorphous in nature, featuring sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon centers, facilitating σ and π bonding. The "top-down technique" (e.g., laser ablation, electrochemical exfoliation, arc-discharge method, oxidative acid treatment) and the "bottom-up approach" (e.g., hydrothermal, thermal combustion, microwave, ultrasonic) are the two primary methods used in the synthesis of CQDs [33] [34]. Hydrothermal synthesis is notable among them because of its low cost, ease of use, and favorable effects on the environment. CQDs are widely used in a variety of applications, including medication delivery [35], bio imaging [36], sensing [37–40] including heavy metals, medications, organic and inorganic pollutants, fingerprint detection [41,42], antibacterial agents, and light-emitting diodes [43]. Further, hydrothermally synthesized CQDs with low cytotoxicity have been employed in imaging various human cells, including SCC-7, A193, A549, MCF-7, and Hep-2 cells [44–47]. Moreover, there is significant interest in utilizing CQDs as fluorescent probes for intracellular imaging and the quantitative detection of specific metal ions in DI water and cells with high sensitivity and selectivity.

In this study, we have synthesized CQDs with multi-colour emission capabilities (MCQDs) via a one-step hydrothermal approach at temperatures of 160°C. The Meliaceae family plant leaves, particularly Swietenia macrophylla, commonly known as mahogany, were used as a precursor. Mahogany, or Swietenia macrophylla, is the popular name for this tree that grows in tropical areas, mostly in South and Central America and Southeast Asia. It is used in medicine. Many people know that it can help with health problems because it has healing qualities like reducing inflammation, killing germs, and lowering blood sugar. Its wide range of phytochemicals, such as flavonoids, tannins, and limonoids, give it these beneficial properties. Because of these features, Swietenia macrophylla leaves provide a long-lasting, low-cost, and useful starting material for the environmentally friendly production of CQDs that could have uses in medicine and the environment [48]. The synthesized MCQDs have the ability to emit multicolour (mainly blue, green, and red) emissions and obtain a maximum of 440 nm. These MCQDs exhibit dual optical sensitivity to both As3+ and Fe3+ ions. When exposed to Fe³ ⁺ ions [49], MCQDs experience fluorescence quenching and form aggregates at a high concentration of 400 μM, facilitating their removal from the water bodies. Conversely, the detection of As3+ ions with MCQDs results in enhanced photoluminescence (PL) emission. Additionally, MCQDs demonstrate biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity and were used in bioimaging applications and detection of As3+ and Fe3+ ions in NIH-3T3 cell lines.

The Mahogany plant leaves, used as precursor, were obtained from the botanical garden of BHU Varanasi in Uttar Pradesh. The following metal salts were acquired from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd.: KCl, NH4Cl, CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl2, and CuCl2. BaCl2 and FeCl3 were supplied by Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd. in India, NaAsO2, and SD Fine Chemical Ltd. in Mumbai, India, respectively. All chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure deionized water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ-cm was used in this study. The mouse fibroblast cell line (NIH-3T3) was provided by the National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS) in Pune, India. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was sourced from Gibco, while high glucose DMEM media, Hi Media provided the antibiotic solution and trypsin solution. MTT (Methylthiazol-2-yl)-3,4,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide) Reagent and DAPI were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd, respectively. The cells were maintained at 37˚C with 5 % CO2 in a humidified CO2 incubator.

The Mahogany leaves were washed with deionized water (DI) and air-dried overnight before cutting into small pieces. Initially, small pieces of leaves from the Mahogani plant were collected in a beaker, then immersed in deionized water at a ratio of 1:2 w/v and heated over a hot plate for 3 hours at 100 °C. The final extract was poured into a 200 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave chamber after being filtered via Whatman filter paper. This chamber was then placed in a muffle furnace at 160 °C for 8 hours. Following the hydrothermal treatment, the synthesized solution was permitted to cool to ambient temperature naturally, resulting in a dark brown-colored solution. The supernatant was subsequently collected by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 15 minutes at 27 °C. The larger particles were removed from this supernatant by filtering it through a 0.2 μm syringe filter. The new filter MCQDs were subsequently collected and stored at 4 °C for future use. The MCQDs solution was lyophilized in a glass tube to obtain the dry weight of MCQDs. A stock solution with a concentration of 8 mg/ml MCQDs was prepared for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental techniques for characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A transmission electron microscope (TEM) equipped with a High-Angle Annular Dark Field (HAADF) detector operating at 200 kV from The FEI TECNAI G2 20 TWIN USA was utilized for imaging purposes. ImageJ software was employed to analyze the high-resolution images. A bio-spectrophotometer (Eppendorf) was employed to conduct UV-Vis absorbance measurements within the 200 nm to 800 nm range. Fluorolog-Horiba fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France) was used for PL emission spectra. Samples were prepared and analyzed in the frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 FT-IR) to identify the MCQDs' functional groups. An Al K-Alpha with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV monochromatic micro-focused X-ray source from Thermo Fisher Scientific was employed to measure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a 10 mA and 15 kV. The Casa XPS software (Casa Software Ltd., United Kingdom) was employed to perform the XPS data analysis. The Zetasizer Pro (Malvern Panalytical) was used to measure the hydrodynamic radius and charge stability of MCQDs CuK radiation at 1.54 keV, 40 kV, and 15 mA was used to study crystallinity with an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Miniflex 600 Desktop X-Ray Diffraction System, Tokyo, Japan). CQD cellular intake was studied using confocal microscopy (Carl Zeiss 780 LSM Laser Scanning Confocal Microscopy System, Germany). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurements were performed with Edinburgh instruments and a picosecond pulse diode LASER. The MTT assay was conducted using an ELISA plate reader (USCNK, China).

The highly sensitive fluorescence probe method was employed to successfully detect toxic heavy metal ions in the study. It was possible to differentiate between the selectivity of Fe3+ and As3+ in distinct aqueous solutions containing various metal ions (Mg2+, Cr2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, As3+, As5+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, and Na+). Each ion solution was prepared from the corresponding salts and stored at a concentration of 100 μM. To assess the selectivity, varying concentrations of Fe3+ and As3+ were introduced to a fixed quantity of MCQD. Following a 5-minute incubation period, the samples were excited at 340 nm to measure fluorescence intensity at different concentrations of analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion sensing of Fe3+ and As3+
ce:section.ce:para:
A final stock solution of CQD was prepared by combining 0.01 ml of MCQD with 4 ml of deionized water in a quartz cuvette, maintaining the pH range between 6 and 7. Here, 2 ml of diluted MCQD solution was combined with 2 ml of the metal solution, which had varying molar concentrations. A control solution was also prepared by mixing 2 ml of diluted MCQD with 2 ml of deionized water. Subsequently, all solutions were allowed to incubate at room temperature for approximately 5 minutes, following which their fluorescence intensity was measured at 340 nm. Notably, heavy metals such as Fe3+ and As3+ exhibited strong interactions with MCQD. The As3+ notably intensified the blue-green emission peak, while Fe3+ caused a quenched blue green emission intensity. The experimental configuration consisted of Fe3+ and As3+ concentrations that varied from 10 μM to 5 mM and 10 μM to 600 μM, respectively [50]. Using the Stern–Volmer relation, the efficacy of the quenching effect observed in the MCQDs was quantified, as described by Eq. (1).

The concentration of metal ions is denoted by Q, while the Stern-Volmer quenching constant is represented by Ksv. The limit of detection (LOD) was subsequently determined using the following Eq. (2).
(2)

Where "σ" represents the standard deviation of the y-intercept and "s" represents the slope of the linear line.

The DPPH method was employed to investigate the free radical scavenging activity and sensing ability of MCQDs. DPPH solution was prepared in dark conditions at 100 μM concentration in methanol. The MCQDs were diluted with double-distilled water to make 1.196 mg/ml stock solutions. To make different concentrations, the stock solution was diluted to 1 ml with water in a variety of volumes. Now, the solutions were added to 2 ml of the produced methanolic DPPH solution to create samples at concentrations from 0 to 0.25 mg/ml in 3 ml. The samples were incubated for 30 minutes in darkness. The Scavenging Activity percentages (%) were calculated using Eq. (3).

where Ac is the DPPH radical absorbance without the sample or standard at 517 nm and As is the absorbance in the presence of sample. The concentrations that inhibited DPPH radicals 50 % were the IC50 values.

The assessment of biocompatibility of the MCQDs was conducted at various concentrations like 30, 90, 180, and 270 µg/ml, and the percentage viability was determined in the NIH-3T3 cell line. We chose the NIH-3T3 cell line due to its wide use in cytotoxicity, bioimaging, and wound healing studies. These mouse embryonic fibroblasts are widely accepted for preliminary biocompatibility and cellular uptake of nanomaterials studies because of their consistent high reproducibility characteristics and sensitivity to environmental changes. Our MCQDs show strong potential for bioimaging, live In-vivo imaging, and a tracking agent in regenerative applications, making them well-suited for reliable in vitro testing. Initially, 5 × 103 cells/well were seeded in a 96-well microliter plate and supplemented with 10 % FBS. Finally, the cells were incubated at 37 °C in an atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 for 24 hours. The cells were exposed to the samples at the specified concentrations for 24, 48, and 72 hours on the following day. To facilitate the formation of formazan crystals, 20 µl of MTT solution (5 mg/ml in PBS with pH 7.4) was added to each well after each time point and incubated at 37 °C for 4 hours. The formazan crystals were subsequently dissolved in 150 µl of DMSO. After combining on a mechanical plate mixer, the optical density (O.D.) was measured at 570 nm using an ELISA reader plate (Synergy HT, BioTek, USA). The measurements were carried out in triplicate. The number of viable cells was directly proportional to the color that was produced. The formula described below was employed to determine the percent (%) viability:
(4)

Where [A]t and [A]c are the absorbance of the Sample and control.

The experiment tested MCQD hemolysis in mouse blood. Whole blood was mixed with an equal volume of PBS at pH 7.4 and then centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 minutes to separate the plasma. The red blood cells were subsequently washed with PBS (pH 7.4), and the supernatant was discarded. This was followed by the suspension of the cleansed red blood cells in an equivalent volume of PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, 200 µL of these cells were added to each microcentrifuge tube, and MCQDs were added. Two and four hours were spent maintaining the containers at room temperature while they were continuously stirred on a shaker. After incubation, the supernatant was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 5 minutes and transferred to a 96-well plate for absorbance measurement at 540 nm. Triton-X (0.1 %) served as the positive control, while PBS pH 7.4 acted as the negative control. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated using the following formula.
(5)

Where, Asample, A saline, A+ control = Absorbance of the sample, negative control, and positive control (Triton-X)

This experiment will reveal MCQD's red blood cell hemolysis [51]. It is expected that MCQD-treated samples will have different absorbance readings than negative control samples but be equivalent to positive control samples.

24-well microplate with full medium, 50,000 NIH-3T3 cells per well were added onto 12 mm circular glass cover slides. Prior to adding MCQDs, cells were pre-treated with 100 μM As3+ and Fe3+ for 30 minutes, and the medium was refilled the next day.

The supernatant was extracted after a 2-hour incubation period, and the cells were washed three times with chilled PBS. After fixing the cells with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 minutes, they were rinsed with PBS to remove any leftover paraformaldehyde. The cell nuclei were stained with DAPI (1 μg/ml), and any excess DAPI was removed by rinsing with chilled PBS. The cover sheets were mounted on slides in DPX and analyzed using a confocal microscope at a magnification of 63x. The excitation wavelengths selected were 359 nm for DAPI and 340 nm for those of the quantum dots.

The in vivo bioimaging potential of MCQD was investigated by an in-vivo imaging system (Photon Imager Optima V4 – Biospace Lab (Photon Acquisition 3.6 software)), and images were analyzed by M 3 Vision analysis software. Briefly, 1 ml of MCQD was administered intravenously to anesthetized Swiss albino mice, and after 15 minutes, the mice was placed on the sample stage of the in vivo imaging system and observed for the distribution of MCQD in the body.

The shape and size of the MCQDs were investigated by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), revealing a predominantly uniform quasi-spherical shape, as shown in Fig. 1(a). The size distribution analysis of MCQDs Fig. 1(b) showed an average size 3.10 ± 0.3 nm. UV-Visible spectroscopy, complementing fluorescence spectroscopy, was employed to ascertain the optical properties of the synthesized MCQDs. The UV-Vis spectrum exhibited two characteristic absorption bands Fig. 1(c), a strong band centered at 286.8 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions of CC bonds, and a broad peak at 340 nm, attributed to n-π* transitions of CO. The photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra of MCQDs recorded at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The emission spectra show characteristic peaks at 440 nm for MCQDs indicating their distinct optical properties.

The XRD pattern of MCQDs, illustrated in Fig. 1(d), displays a broad peak with less pronounced features at 22° and 29° [52], this peak is attributed to the (002) crystal plane, indicative of the graphite-like structure characteristic of the MCQDs. The peak at 42° is attributed to (100) planes of graphitic structure [53]. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) data were obtained for the MCQDs with an excitation wavelength of 385 nm. The decay characteristics of 485 nm emission peaks were measured. PL decay curves were fitted with bi-exponential functions. (Eq. 5) yielding double lifetimes (τ1& τ2) Figure S1(a). The lifetimes for the emission peaks at 485 nm were determined to be τavg 0.37 (Eq. 6).

By using different long pass optical filters Figure S1(b), we recorded the blue, green, and red color emissions of MCQDs under 340 nm excitation in photoluminescence measurements. Figure S1(c) depicts MCQDs treated with and without UV light. Without UV light, the solution appears brownish, while under UV light, it exhibits a luminescent blue fluorescence.

To further investigate in the interaction process, MCQDs were measured using FTIR and XPS with the addition of Fe³ ⁺ and As³ ⁺ ions. The FTIR spectra of the MCQDs Fig. 2(a) show broad peaks at 3422 cm−1 corresponding to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, a sharp absorption peak at 2922 cm−1 assigned to C-H stretching vibrations, and peaks at 1736 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 for CO stretching vibrations (carbonyl groups) corresponding to CC stretching vibrations or stretching vibrations of aromatic rings [54]. Additionally, peaks at 1072 cm−1 and 1258 cm−1 are observed for O-CO and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively. After the addition of Fe3+ and As3+ ions, no significant changes were observed in the vibrational spectra. These results show that the MCQDs have nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon on its surface. Surface oxygen and nitrogen functional groups are present on the MCQDs, making them water-soluble due to their high oxygen functional group content. Furthermore, the emission peak position shifted towards longer wavelengths, accompanied by a decrease in PL intensity as the excitation wavelength varied from 340 nm to 440 nm, Fig. 2(c).

The photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the synthesized MCQDs were studied at fixed concentrations (100 µM) of various metal ions, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Significant fluorescent intensity changes were observed in the PL at 440 nm after the addition of the metal ions. These changes were subsequently quantified, as shown in Fig. 2(d), where bar graphs effectively illustrate the variations in peak intensities for different toxic metals at 440 nm, and Fo and F are the PL intensities of MCQDs in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively.

Remarkably, different behaviors were observed in response to Fe3+ and As3+ ions. In particular, confinement of Fe3+ ions resulted in rapid quenching of the 440 nm peak, whereas As3+ ions caused enhancement of the same peak.

This phenomenon makes mahogany leaf-derived MCQDs as potential ON-OFF sensors with high specificity for Fe3+ and As3+ ions in aqueous matrices. The study conducted on the detailed photoluminescence (PL) evaluations of MCQDs concerning varying concentrations of Fe3+ and As3+ ions provided a thorough understanding of their sensing capabilities. By analyzing the PL emission spectra of MCQDs in Fig. 3(a) at 340 nm excitation wavelength for different Fe3+ ions concentrations, it was observed that the peak intensity at 440 nm decreased with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration, becoming completely extinguished at Fe3+ ion concentrations of 5 mM or higher, Fig. 3(b) The Stern Volmer (SV) plot illustrated the relationship between Fo /F and Fe3+ concentration, where Fo and F are MCQD's 440 nm peak PL intensity without and with Fe3+ ions, respectively. The linear correlation observed between the Fo/F ratio and Fe3+ ion concentrations within the range of 10 µM to 100 µM Fig. 3(c) facilitated the determination of the Limit of Detection (LOD), which was calculated to be 8.07 Nm.

Conversely, the peak intensity at 440 nm exhibited an enhancement upon the addition of As3+ ions Fig. 3(d). The Stern Volmer (SV) plot IN Fig. 3(e) shows the fluctuation of Fo/F as a function of As3+ concentration. From 10 µM to 50 µM concentration range, the semi-logarithmic curve shows a linear change in gain for As3+ ions. This peculiar behavior facilitated the determination of a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.36 nM for As3+ ions Fig. 3(f). Table 1 compares the data for LOD values and linear range for the ions As3+ and Fe3+ with published values from the literature. The LOD values match or exceed those seen in the literature. The PL investigations emphasize the significant potential of MCQDs made from Mahogany leaves as effective luminous probes for the sensitive and precise detection of Fe3+ and As3+ ions in aquatic environments. We recorded the PL spectra of MCQD under the presence of both Fe3+ and As3+ ions Fig. S6(a) to differentiate between the Fe3+ and As3+ ions in the same solution. While Fe3+ concentration was increased from 10 μM to 150 μM, As3+ ion concentration was fixed at 10 μM. We found that the rate of quenching Fe3+ decreases with the presence of As3+ ions. This is because the presence of As3+ ions in the solution may increase the PL intensity of the MCQDs as described earlier. This enhancement can influence Fe3+ ion quenching. Plot Fig. S6(b) presents a linear plot of Fo/F as a function of Fe3+ ions where Fo and F are the 440 nm peak PL intensity without and with Fe3+ ions, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed to investigate the chemical composition of the synthesized MCQDs. Figs. 4(a), 4(c), and 4(e) shows the deconvolution of C1s XPS spectra for MCQDs, MCQDs+Fe3+ and MCQDs+As3+ and illustrate the C1s XPS spectrum, revealing distinct bonding configurations including C-C, CC, C-O, C-N, CO, and O-CO [64] consistent with those observed in MCQDs. However, notable variations in the concentration of these species were observed. With the introduction of As3+ and Fe3+ ions, the concentration of the graphitic species CC decreased from approximately 38 % to around 18 % and 25 %, respectively. Conversely, the concentration of the graphitic species C-C increased from 23 % to approximately 34 % and 37 % for As3+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. Other functional groups did not exhibit significant changes Table ST2(a).

Figs. 4(b), 4(d), and 4(f) shows the deconvolution of O1s XPS spectra for MCQDs, MCQDs+Fe3+ and MCQDs+As3+ respectively. It was observed that with the addition of As3+ ions, the CO/C-O and C-O-C bond show changes in the % area. CO/C-O bond shows ∼19 % decrease, and C-O-C shows ∼ 15 % increase in % area, i.e., these two bonds are mainly responsible for sensing of As3+ ions and other constituent O-O, C-O-H shows no significant changes. Upon the addition of Fe3+, the CO/C-O decreases by ∼18 %, and C-OH increases by ∼15 %, and other spectra remain the same without any significant changes Table ST2 (b), i.e., these two bonds are mainly responsible for sensing of Fe3+ ions. Similarly, the deconvolution of N1s of MCQs, with metal ions (As3+ and Fe3+), Figure S3 (d), (e), and (f). We observed that the C-N/CN decreased from ∼ 89 % to ∼ 58 % after the addition of As3+ in MCQDs, and a new functional group appeared like graphitic-N (∼ 14 %), pyridinic-N (∼ 20 %), and metal nitrides (∼6 %). But the addition of Fe3+ ions results in no significant increase of the C-N/CN (∼89 % to ∼92 %), and there is an appearance of a new functional group graphitic-N (∼7 %) Table ST2 (c)). From the above discussion, we can conclude that C and O-containing functional groups are participating in the As3+ and Fe3+ sensing mechanisms.

Figure S3(a) shows XPS survey spectrum of MCQDs containing peaks of C1s, O1s, and N1s and Fe3+ and As3+ ions. Figures S2(a), (b), and (c) show that the peak position of C1s shifted towards higher binding energy (284 eV) when metal ions (10 μM) are added to the MCQDs (20 μg/ml) solution. This shift is accompanied by a change in peak shape attributed to the increased graphitic contribution (288.6 eV). When As3+ ions are present, O1 peak positions change towards lower binding energy. The N1s peaks show no substantial alterations. The high-resolution XPS spectra of As3d and Fe2p are illustrated in Figure. S3(b) and (c), respectively.

Validation involved testing real water samples for Fe3+ and As3+ using MCQDs. Water from RO, INST Mohali Lake, Pawai Lake Maharastra, Ganga River Varanasi, Kharagpur Lake, Varanasi Field, and lab DI water were used to sample real water. All water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter and heated to 100 °C for 5 minutes to remove biological pollutants and preserve a pH near 7 before testing. The MCQD probe was used to measure the water samples' fluorescence and compare it to the deionized water Fig. 5(a). It can be observed that the Pawai's lake water fluoresced most, followed by Ganga River, field, DI, and RO water. The addition of 10 µL of MCQD to all Samples led to an increase in the peak intensity at 440 nm Fig. 5(b). Comparing the emission spectra of water samples with MCQDs to those in DI water revealed an increase in the 440-nm peak intensity for most samples except field water and KGP water. A bar graph in Fig. 5(c) visually represents these changes, indicating the presence of Fe3+ ions presence in field water and As3+ ions in other water samples where Iwith and Iwithout are the PL intensity of samples with and Without MCQDs.

To further confirm that the observed fluorescence responses in real water samples (Figs. 5a and 5b) are specifically due to interactions between MCQDs and Fe³ ⁺/As³ ⁺ ions rather than matrix interference, spiking experiments were conducted. For Fe³ ⁺ sensing, known concentrations of Fe³ ⁺ (50 µM and 100 µM) were added to field water samples, while for As³ ⁺ sensing, 10 µM and 20 µM of As³ ⁺ were added to Pawai Lake water samples. In both cases, the expected changes in photoluminescence were observed and quenching of PL intensity upon Fe³ ⁺ addition and enhancement upon As³ ⁺ addition Figure S7(a&b). These consistent responses confirm the selective recognition ability of MCQDs toward Fe³ ⁺ and As³ ⁺ ions. Additionally, all samples were pre-treated by filtration and dilution to minimize background interference, further supporting that the fluorescence modulation arises from specific MCQD–metal ion interactions.

Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy was used to verify the dynamic quenching mechanism in the sensor. We observed the kinetics of quenching the Photoluminescence (PL) emission peak excited at 385 nm, which was observed at 440 nm. A second order exponential decay function was used to fit the lifetime decay plots by using Eq. (5).

In the As3+ sensing system Fig. 5(d), we investigated the dynamics of the PL emission peak at 440 nm under an excitation wavelength of 385 nm. A noticeable increase in lifetime was observed as a function of concentration. The data were fitted with a bi-exponential function, revealing that the addition of As3+ led to an average lifetime increase from 0.37 to 0.58 ns. Furthermore, the values of τ1 and τ2 increased from 0.35 ns to 0.15 ns and from 2.69 ns to 0.89 ns, respectively. The TRPL data in Fig. 5(e) showed two decay constants, τ1 and τ2, for different quenching species. In the control case, τ1 and τ2 were 0.35 and 2.69 ns, respectively, with an average lifetime (τavg) of 0.37 ns. The nonlinear SV plot depicted in Fig. 3(b) for Fe3+ ions suggest the coexistence of static and dynamic quenching. The average lifetime fell from 0.37 ns to 0.23 ns as Fe3+ ion concentration rose, showing dynamic quenching in the sensing mechanism. The corresponding values of τ1 τ2, τavg, and the ratio of τ0/τavg for Fe3+ ions and As3+ ions are presented in Table ST1. Additionally, the variation of average lifetime as a function of Fe3+ and As3+ ion concentration is depicted in Fig. 5(f).

6.0 × 105 M−1 for Fe3+ ions. This high value of KD indicates high fluorescence quenching efficiency.

The MCQDs' UV-Vis spectra were obtained following the addition of Fe3+ metal ions in order to examine the possibility of static quenching. One important aspect of static quenching is the creation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the quencher and the fluorophore. The UV-Vis spectra of the MCQDs following the addition of Fe3+ ions were examined in order to verify the creation of any non-fluorescent complexes. Fig. 6(a) illustrates the UV-Vis absorption spectra of MCQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions ranging from 10 µM to 800 µM. It is observed that the intensity of the peaks around 280 nm diminishes upon the addition of Fe3+ ions, resulting in the appearance of a hump around 360 nm. This corresponds to the overall decrease in fluorescence intensity observed in the PL data. Thus, the changes in UV-Vis absorption spectra upon the addition of Fe3+ ions suggest the formation of a new ground state complex between the MCQDs and Fe3+ ions, which impedes the usual fluorescence centers. Fig. 6(b) demonstrates the solution of MCQDs added to Fe3+ ions (ranging from 10 µM to 1 mM) under both daylight and UV light. As the concentration of Fe3+ ions increases up to 1 mM, complex aggregate structures form. The aggregation of Fe3+ ions is further confirmed under UV light by the emission of green fluorescence exclusively from the aggregated Fe3+, while the rest of the solution remains clear. This indicates a strong binding affinity between MCQDs and Fe3+ ions, leading to the disappearance of the absorption peak around 280 nm with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration. The absorption peak at 280 nm nearly vanishes, while the hump around the peak at 360 nm may be attributed to the aggregation of Fe3+ ions. In the case of the MCQDs with the addition of As3+ metal ions (with a concentration of 100 µM to 500 µM), minimal changes were observed in the UV-Vis spectra compared to the control MCQDs at 282 nm. However, a new hump formation around 346 nm emerged, particularly evident for the concentration 300 µM to 500 µM Fig. 6(c). Additionally, MCQDs added to As3+ solutions exhibited a noticeable color change behavior from 100 µM to 1 mM. These changes, particularly around 300 µM to 1 mM, were also visible when the samples were exposed to UV light, resulting in green fluorescence emission Fig. 6(d). This suggests the formation of new metal complexes upon the addition of As3+ions, supported by the presence of a hump at 346 nm and the observed color-changing pattern from 300 µM to 1 mM, potentially contributing to its intensity enhancement in photoluminescence. The TRPL analysis shows a significant increase in fluorescence lifetime upon interaction with As³ ⁺ ions, indicating surface passivation and the formation of emissive metal-ligand complexes [65]. UV-Vis spectroscopy confirmed the emergence of a new absorption hump at 346 nm, accompanied by a notable redshift in the photoluminescence emission with increasing As³ ⁺ concentrations, suggesting to change MCQDs electronic environment due to complex formation. These changes, along with visible fluorescence enhancement under UV light, particularly at higher As³ ⁺ concentrations (≥300 µM), highlight the formation of new ground state complexes. The observed fluorescence redshift and lifetime enhancement emphasize the dynamic interaction between As³ ⁺ ions and the MCQDs, enhancing photoluminescence and enabling a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensing mechanism [66].

In the presence of metal ions, MCQDs' zeta potential was measured as well. The zeta potential of MCQDs with and without metal ions is shown in Figure S5. The zeta potential of the produced MCQDs devoid of metal ions was −25.85 mV, suggesting the presence of surface functional groups rich in electrons that provide stability in the aqueous solution. When Fe3+ (10 µM) and As3+ (10 µM) ions were added, the zeta potential dropped to −5.23 mV and −16.85 mV, respectively. The interaction between the functional groups and the ions Fe3+ and As3+ is responsible for the decrease in the surface charge of MCQDs.

When MCQDs are mixed with Fe3+, it makes aggregation and settles down in a few minutes. We can easily remove this aggregation by using a centrifuge (10000 rpm for 1 min) and 0.2 μm syringe filter. The validation of the removal of Fe3+ is also confirmed by PL measurement. In PL intensity of MCQDs peak gets quenched when Fe3+ is mixed with MCQDs and makes aggregates and records their PL emission spectra. We observe a strong collision quenching in the PL emission spectra. After filtering out the aggregates by using 0.2 mm syringe filter, the emission peak moved towards the control emission peak. This confirmed the removal of iron aggregations from the solution. The remaining MCQDs show their emission spectra. This confirms the MCQDs as novel detector and binder towards the Fe3+ ions. The return of fluorescence after the removal of the aggregates complex, as shown in Fig. 7(a), occurs because the aggregation of MCQDs with Fe³ ⁺ ions causes collisional quenching of photoluminescence. When the aggregates are removed through centrifugation and filtration, collisional quenching is eliminated, and the remaining MCQDs give their intrinsic fluorescence.

Figure S4 shows the stability of MCQDs, systematically studied with respect to variations in pH, exposure to UV light, extended storage time, and salinity. Photoluminicent spectroscopy Figure S4 (a), (b), (c) UV–vis absorption spectra Figure S4 (d), (e), (f) and Zeta potential techniques were used in this investigation. When irradiated with UV light from 0 to 3 hours, the MCQDs showed robust stability in PL, absorption spectra and Zeta potential. In addition, the MCQDs showed considerable resistance to varying salt levels.When the pH range was varied from 3 to 9, a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity was observed at ∼ 410 nm. This trend coincided with a decrease in absorbance in the UV-Vis spectrum as well as in zeta potential also. The response of Zeta and fluorescence to pH changes is attributed to the reversible process of protonation and deprotonation involving the functional groups present on the MCQDs.In addition, the effect of aging on the stability of MCQDs was investigated. Figure. S5(d) shows the photoluminescence emission (PL) of the MCQDs observed over a period of one year. During this period, no significant changes were seen in the PL spectra of the MCQDs. Overall, these results highlight the exceptional stability of the MCQDs across a spectrum of environmental conditions. This exceptional stability positions the MCQDs as promising candidates for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant, MTT, and bioimaging of CQDs on NIH-3T3
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen-centered free radical DPPH takes hydrogen atoms to create DPPH-H and changes from deep violet to light yellow when it interacts with antioxidants[67]. The remaining DPPH radical in solution was assessed by measuring absorbance at 519 nm against a blank, and the scavenging activity percentage was computed.As the concentration of MCQDs gradually increased from 0.028 to 0.196 mg/ml, a corresponding decrease in absorbance intensity was observed, indicating its free radical scavenging activity Fig. 8(a). The inset of Fig. 8(a) shows percentage scavenging of DPPH radicals and the IC50 value obtained from the related Eq. 3, was determined to be 0.155 mg/ml.in Figure S5(f) the DPPH solution changing from deep violet to light yellow, supporting this finding. The hemolysis assay tested MCQDs' ex-vivo biocompatibility by incubating them with mice blood for 2 and 4 hours. All experimental groups had less hemolysis than Triton-X, showing that MCQDs are biocompatible with red blood cells at both time intervals Fig. 8(b). Cytotoxicity was measured using the MTT test on NIH-3T3 cells exposed to MCQDs at doses of 30, 90, 180, and 270 µg/ml for 24, 48, and 72 hours. Fig. 8(c) and (d) show MCQDs' dose- and time-dependent cytotoxicity and viability in NIH-3T3 cells at 24, 48, and 72 h. Minimal cytotoxicity was observed at varying concentrations after 24 and 48 h, with less than 20 % cytotoxicity even at higher concentrations after 72 hours. The cell viability of MCQDs in NIH-3T3 cell lines remained high (80–90 %) across the different concentrations and time points, confirming the biocompatibility of MCQDs even at elevated concentrations.

Further, we have used biocompatible MCQD for fluorescent-based sensing of Fe3+ and As3+ ions in cells. The fluorescent-based sensing depends on analyzing fluorescence intensity of MCQD in relation to heavy metals such as Fe3+ and As3+ in cells Figure S5(e). After the addition of Fe3+ ions, a non-fluorescent fluorophore forms, which alters the fluorescence behavior. On the other hand, the electron transfer process increases the PL intensity in the case of As3+ ions. It is possible to verify the conjugation of Fe3+ and As3+ with MCQD and, consequently, the presence of these metals in the cells by analyzing fluctuations in fluorescence. The fluorescence changes that occur from Fe3+ and As3+ conjugation with MCQD in pre-treated NIH-3T3 cells are shown in Fig. 9. It is evident that MCQD exhibited strong self-fluorescence in both the cytoplasm and the nucleus. However, Fe3+ and As3+ do not exhibit any self-fluorescence. The treatment of cells with As3+ and MCQD results in an increase in green fluorescence and quenching of red fluorescence intensity as compared to MCQD alone. When compared to MCQD alone, treatment of cells with Fe3+ and MCQD demonstrated a greater quenching of green fluorescence intensity with essentially minimal red fluorescence. The evaluation above suggests that Fe3+ and As3+ heavy metals can be found in a system using this fluorescence-based biosensing method.

The in vivo bio imaging potential of MCQDs was investigated by an in vivo imaging system. The In Vivo Imaging system (IVIS) images from Fig. 10 (a-e) depict that MCQDs have predominantly distributed in the major organs of the Swiss albino mice viz., lungs, liver intestines, and bladder after 15 minutes of intravenous administration of MCQDs. The intensity of bioluminescence signals in different regions of interest has been depicted in the graph Fig. 10 (f). The results demonstrated in the Figures and graph clearly postulate that MCQDs could be explored as a bio-imaging tool in the healthcare sector to target different organs of the body.

In this study, we successfully synthesized mahogany leaf-derived Multicolour Carbon Quantum Dots (MCQDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method, presenting an efficient fluorometric sensor for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and As3+ ions. The present fluorometric sensing approach exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, with reported detection limits of 8.07 nM for Fe3+ and 2.36 nM for As3+ ions in deionized water.

Our proposed sensing method demonstrated promising results in real water systems, offering significant implications for environmental monitoring. Fluorescence lifetime measurements indicated that the quenching of fluorescence upon Fe3+ ion addition resulted from a combination of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms. Notably, a high dynamic quenching constant (KD = 6.0 ×105 M−1 for Fe3+ ions) underscored the efficient fluorescence quenching capability of the system. Investigating the interaction of metal ions with MCQDs via XPS revealed that Fe3+ and As3+ ions bind to oxygen functional groups on the MCQD surface, consequently altering the fluorescence properties.

The synthesized MCQD displayed remarkable stability in various environmental conditions, including pH, salt concentrations, and storage conditions. Further experimentation involved the application of MCQDs for detecting Fe3+ and As3+ ions in a mouse fibroblast cell line. Additionally, a positive hemolysis study was conducted, affirming the biocompatibility of MCQDs with minimal cytotoxic effects. These findings underscore the potential of MCQDs as versatile probes for biomedical applications, including cellular imaging and sensing.

In summary, our study presents a comprehensive exploration of mahogany leaf-derived MCQDs as multifunctional materials with promising applications in environmental monitoring, biomedical imaging, and sensing. The successful synthesis and characterization of MCQDs, coupled with their remarkable stability, sensitivity, and biocompatibility, position them as valuable tools for various scientific and technological endeavors.
